title: Only one podman container can use host volume at a time
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST?**:

 **kind bug**

**Description**
When using a host volume in a container, access is fine. If another container attempts to access a subdirectory, the original container can no longer access the host volume.
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a directory with random folders and files on the host. 

2. Create container and use mount or -v to attach to the host directory

3. Create a second container that -v 's a subdirectory (or same directory) on the host

**Describe the results you received:**
First container loses access to the volume (generic permission denied messages). Restarting the container will then steal access from the second container.

**Describe the results you expected:**
Both containers can access the same files/folders on the host. This is the behaviour in Docker.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
This circumstance may not be in use by lots of people, I use it namely for letsencyrpt at home (at work it's all OpenShift now). So I have a container that does the periodic check and update of the certificate. A apache container and postfix container both attach to the directory to read the certificate (only the letsencrypt container as rw).

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 0.12.1.2

```

**Output of `podman info`:**


**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Physical (Intel NUC 5i7ryb)

comments.body:
Can you try disabling SELinux (`setenforce 0`) to see if that resolves the issue?

If so, you should investigate the volume SELinux options (`:z` in particular, to indicate the volume is shared by two containers) to tell Podman to mark the volume as accessed by multiple containers.

Sorry, I did forget to mention that. I did use :Z, the files show as having container_file_t applied. I can try with selinux in permissive or disabled later today, but the audit log showed no denials as well, as I had been watching it

Can you try lowercase Z? Capital Z uses a per-container private label, which would prevent containers sharing volumes.

It would be very interesting if it's not SELinux, though. These are just bind mounts, and we're not even making them (`runc` is). If it turns out to not be SELinux, can you try Docker on the same host and see if you see the same problem?

If you need more then one container to share a volume then use the lower case :z.

man podman run
...
To change a label in the container context, you can add either of two suffixes :z or :Z to the volume mount. These suffixes tell podman to relabel file objects on the shared volumes. The z option tells podman that two containers share the volume content. As a result, podman labels the content with a shared content label. Shared volume labels allow all containers to read/write content.  The Z option tells podman to label the content with a private unshared label.  Only the current container can use a private volume.
